author: Eivind Kjosbakken

title: Stop ‘tokenmaxxing’ and deploy AI sensibly instead
content:
Nature Machine Intelligence, Published online: 18 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s42256-026-01253-5

Companies, tech workers and researchers are in a frenzy to embed agentic AI into their workflows, locked in a self-imposed race not to fall behind. There must be a better way to make use of AI technology.

title: Plagiarism of ideas in the age of generative artificial intelligence
content:
Nature Machine Intelligence, Published online: 18 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s42256-026-01247-3

Generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) tools are challenging our understanding of plagiarism. How should we deal with plagiarism of ideas if this misbehaviour is increasingly common, and it is extremely difficult to prove when GenAI is involved? Definitions of research misconduct that specifically address the use of GenAI tools are needed.
author: David Resnik, Mohammad Hosseini

title: SpecGP as a transformer-based model for predicting energy-adaptable structural spectra of glycopeptides
content:
Nature Machine Intelligence, Published online: 18 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s42256-026-01246-4

SpecGP enhances fragment ion coverage to enable the prediction of N-glycopeptide structural spectra across diverse collision energies, thereby improving isomer discrimination and boosting identification confidence through rescoring.
author: Xianyong Wang, Rui Song, Zhuangzhuang Feng, Shisheng Sun

title: Immunotherapy drug target identification using machine learning and patient-derived tumour explant validation
content:
Nature Machine Intelligence, Published online: 18 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s42256-026-01201-3

Augustine et al. present a multimodal graph neural network that identifies cancer immunotherapy targets. It distinguishes approved and prospective targets, and promising candidates are validated using a clinically relevant patient-derived platform.
author: Marcellus Augustine, Nuno Rocha Nene, Hongchang Fu, Christopher L. Pinder, Lorena Ligammari, Alexander P. Simpson, Irene Sanz-Fernández, Krupa Thakkar, Danwen Qian, Evelyn Fitzsimons, Benjamin S. Simpson, Roberto Vendramin, Andrea Castro, Heather Niederer, Samra Turajlic, Sergio A. Quezada, Nicholas McGranahan, Chris Watkins, Charles Swanton, Kevin Litchfield

title: Radiometric Calibration of OLI/OLI-2 Reflective Solar Bands Using the Moon
content: The operational land imager (OLI) instruments onboard the Landsat 8 and 9 spacecrafts are equipped with a suite of onboard calibration capabilities to meet stringent requirements and continue the high-quality data products of the previous Landsat missions. Continuous radiometric monitoring is essential for maintaining data consistency across the Landsat archive spanning multiple decades. In addition to regular views of the solar diffuser and onboard lamps, the reflective solar bands also view the Moon routinely to improve the radiometric characterization parameters derived from onboard calibration sources. Lunar observations provide an independent, stable, and well-characterized calibration target for long-term trending and cross-calibration between instruments. This article describes the detailed methodology of lunar calibration acquisition, processing, and performance for both OLI instruments. The lunar calibration approach leverages scheduled monthly maneuvers during which the spacecraft is oriented to image the Moon at carefully controlled phase angles (5°–9°). Data processing addresses critical corrections including bias subtraction, FPM-dependent oversampling (factor ~8 varying by 2.4% across the focal plane), and robotic lunar observatory (ROLO) model normalization for viewing geometry. Results demonstrate subpercent precision in tracking on-orbit radiometric changes: visible (VIS) and NIR bands show excellent stability (<0.5%/year variations) with detectable UV-induced degradation in coastal aerosol (CA) and blue bands (0.07%/year–0.38%/year), while SWIR bands exhibit systematic ±0.5% seasonal oscillations requiring further investigation. Comparison with pristine solar diffuser measurements validates lunar-derived trends within 0.5% for most bands, and cross-comparison between Landsat 8 and 9 confirms measurement consistency. This lunar calibration capability enhances confidence in long-term radiometric stability and supports the mission’s climate data record objectives.
author: Amit Angal, Jeffrey Miller, Kurtis Thome

title: From Sparse Satellite Images to Continuous Visual Sequence: A Collaborative Diffusion Framework for Internal Solitary Waves
content: Internal solitary waves (ISWs) are a significant oceanic phenomenon, particularly in the South China Sea (SCS). The propagation of ISWs influences the transport of matter, energy, and momentum within the ocean. They also pose a considerable risk to offshore engineering and submarine safety. To leverage the advantages of ISWs and to mitigate the associated risks to offshore activities, it is valuable to understand their continuous propagation process over a period of time. At present, there is a lack of tools capable of illustrating the continuous propagation of ISWs. Consequently, reconstructing the continuous propagation process of ISWs based on sparse remote sensing imagery is a valuable endeavor. To address this issue, this article defines the completion of the ISW propagation process as an end-to-end visual generation task and proposes a novel collaborative diffusion framework that directly generates high-fidelity visual sequences of ISW propagation. The core innovation of the framework is the disentanglement of static appearance and dynamic motion, together with the integration of physical information into both representations. It employs a dual-path architecture where an appearance model learns the static morphology of ISWs from a single image, while a motion model learns their propagation kinematics. These two components are synergistically constrained by a shared-weights mechanism and physical information, ensuring a physically coherent spatiotemporal representation. The proposed framework is validated using numerical simulation data, synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imagery, and geostationary high-speed imager (GHI) imagery. The framework is assessed primarily by visual metrics, supplemented by quantitative metrics where continuous sequences are available. Quantitative results on both GHI and numerical simulations demonstrate the high fidelity of the proposed method. For the GHI data, the errors in propagation velocity remain within 0.7 m/s, while the predicted propagation direction is highly consistent with the observations. For the numerical simulations, the velocity error is only 0.1 m/s, with a direction deviation of 11°. The results confirm that this framework provides a novel and effective tool for reconstructing the propagation process of ISWs over time from otherwise sparse observations.
author: Zhijie An, B. Saheya, Maoguo Gong, Xiaofeng Li

title: Decision-Level Road Network Fusion From Multisource Remote Sensing Data via Evidential Reasoning Rule and Topology Constraints
content: Recent decades have seen significant advances in road network extraction via artificial intelligence. However, single-source approaches remain vulnerable to sensor-specific limitations, such as occlusions in optical imagery. While multisource fusion can mitigate these gaps, sensor heterogeneity and the lack of well-registered, well-labeled multisource datasets make establishing a unified fusion strategy difficult. In this article, we propose a generic framework for fusing road information derived from multisource remote sensing (RS) images at the decision level, enabling effective combination of outputs from diverse single-source machine learning (ML) models. The presented method consists of two stages. First, to address redundancy (RD) from repeated extractions, we propose a geometric rule-based fusion strategy, featuring a novel nonmaximum suppression (NMS) algorithm tailored for linear targets. Second, evidence from different sources is aggregated using the evidential reasoning (ER) rule, guided by each ML model’s performance and the confidence maps of their outputs. The final fusion result is achieved by optimizing graph labeling through an energy function that integrates the ER rule with topological constraints (TCs). The proposed framework effectively accommodates heterogeneous inputs and is validated on multitemporal and multimodal RS images. Experimental results demonstrate marked improvements in road network completeness (CP) and correctness (CR) over single-source extraction, while also outperforming representative decision-level fusion methods in overall quality (QL) with reduced RD.
author: Fanghong Xiao, Ling Tong, Shiyu Luo, Yuchuan Wang, Jiaxing Yang

title: Dynamic Semantic Graph-Guided Cross-Modal Attention Network for Open-Vocabulary Object Detection in Aerial Images
content: Open-vocabulary object detection (OVD) in aerial images is critical for intelligent perception of multicategory objects in complex scenarios, yet still faces two key challenges: 1) the prevalence of long-tailed categories in aerial image domains leads to insufficient visual-semantic correlation modeling for novel classes due to annotation scarcity and 2) complex backgrounds induce feature confusion and degrade the robustness of cross-modal semantic alignment. To address these issues, we propose a dynamic semantic graph-guided cross-modal attention (DynaGraph-CrossAtt) Network for aerial open-vocabulary detection. The overall architecture of DynaGraph-CrossAtt is collaboratively constituted by two core components, i.e., a dynamic semantic-aware graph (DSG) convolutional module and a bidirectional cross-modal attention (BiCMA) mechanism. In particular, DSG constructs text semantic graphs to propagate fine-grained semantic relationships (e.g., part interactions and spatial dependencies) and dynamically updates graph node representations, thereby enhancing the contextual awareness and discriminability of text embedding, effectively alleviating the representation deficiency of novel classes. Subsequently, BiCMA receives the enhanced text semantic embedding output by DSG and performs cross-modal interaction with vision features. In BiCMA, the vision-to-text branch employs dynamic weight allocation to emphasize critical semantic nodes, while the text-to-vision branch leverages semantic graphs to guide vision feature focusing on object regions, suppressing background noise, forming a dynamic soft-alignment paradigm of “semantic-guided localization and vision feedback optimization” for cross-modal features. Experiments on aerial benchmarks demonstrate that DynaGraph-CrossAtt significantly improves OVD performance in complex aerial image scenarios.
author: Lingjun Li, Xinxin Zhang, Xiaoxu Feng, Yanbu Guo, Shigang Liu, Yali Peng, Xiwen Yao

title: A UB-ETDA-Based Spaceborne SAR Parameter Design Method for Enhanced Extended Target Detection Performance
content: For extended target detection, most existing spaceborne synthetic aperture radar (SAR) parameter design methods primarily focus on ensuring normal system operation, with limited consideration of the impact of SAR parameters on extended target detection performance. To enhance the extended target detection performance of spaceborne SAR from a parameter design perspective, a novel parameter design method based on an upper bound on extended target detection accuracy (UB-ETDA) is proposed, which comprehensively accounts for key factors affecting extended target detection performance, including aspect-dependent scattering characteristics of the extended target and spatial correlation properties of clutter. By employing UB-ETDA as a new performance metric to guide spaceborne SAR parameter design, the extended target detection performance of the system can be effectively improved. First, a statistical signal model for spaceborne SAR extended target detection is developed, systematically characterizing the stochastic properties of the extended target, background clutter, and noise components in SAR images. Then, the expression of UB-ETDA for SAR system is rigorously derived, along with a stable and statistically efficient numerical computation method. Finally, with UB-ETDA as the primary performance metric and existing performance metrics as constraints, a spaceborne SAR parameter design method is proposed for improving extended target detection performance. Simulation results validate the effectiveness of the proposed method.
author: Huaping Xu, Chonghang Wang, Jingwen Li, Bing Sun, Wei Liu, Xianghua Liu

title: t-Product-Based Low-Rank Discriminative Graph Embedding for Robust Dimensionality Reduction of Hyperspectral Images
content: Graph embedding (GE) methods have shown promising performance in the dimensionality reduction (DR) of hyperspectral images (HSIs). However, overreliance on local information while neglecting global structure makes neighborhood graph construction highly susceptible to noise interference, and the projection operators in matrix form for DR may result in the loss of high-order structural information. To address these issues, we propose a t-product-based low-rank discriminative graph embedding (t-LRDGE) model. Specifically, we first integrate tensor-product (t-product) operations with a customized third-order tensor set representation to efficiently capture the intrinsic structure of hyperspectral data. Subsequently, we incorporate tensor low-rank decomposition into the GE for denoising, while considering the global and local information of the data to generate robust low-dimensional data. Finally, we introduce prior discriminative constraints on semantic information to further enhance the discriminative power of the model. In addition, we have developed an iterative algorithm based on the alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMMs) for solving t-LRDGE. Extensive experiments conducted on three datasets demonstrate that t-LRDGE outperforms existing state-of-the-art models in terms of both discriminative capability and robustness, effectively leveraging global and local information while preserving the intrinsic spatial–spectral structure. The source code is available at: https://github.com/loh0324/t-LRDGE
author: Feng Yang, Ouhan Lin, Anyong Qin, Tiecheng Song, Fulin Luo, Chenqiang Gao

title: Joint Low-Rank and Smooth Tensor Regularization for Multispectral and Hyperspectral Image Fusion
content: The fusion of multispectral and hyperspectral images (HSIs) has become an essential technique for generating images with simultaneously high spatial and spectral resolutions. However, this task is inherently an ill-posed inverse problem, and its solution typically relies on incorporating prior knowledge to constrain the search space. Conventional approaches often adopt an additive combination of low-rankness and smoothness priors [e.g., minimization of nuclear norm and total variation (TV)]. While effective to a certain extent, this additive formulation fails to fully capture the intrinsic coupling between the two priors and is highly sensitive to the choice of tradeoff parameters. To address these issues, we propose a weighted low-rank and smooth tensor (WLRST) regularization framework for fusing multispectral and HSIs. Our WLRST leverages the fact that the original image and its gradient maps share similar manifold structures while exhibiting different smoothness levels across spatial–spectral modes. It imposes tensor nuclear norm (TNN) constraints on multimode gradient maps, with adaptively assigned weights that reflect the smoothness variation in each mode. This unified regularizer jointly embeds low-rank and smoothness properties, allowing more precise exploitation of spatial and spectral correlations in the fused image without extensive parameter tuning. Experimental results on benchmark datasets demonstrate that our WLRST method produces significantly finer fusion results compared with state-of-the-art techniques, achieving superior visual quality and improvements across multiple quantitative metrics.
author: Fan Li, Zhaozhao Zeng, Yuyun Liang, Mengxiong Tang, Shaoquan Zhang, Hesong Hu, Jun Li

title: Hierarchical Frequency–Spatial Integration Network for Remote Sensing Super-Resolution
content: Remote sensing image (RSI) super-resolution (SR) offers a cost-effective and efficient approach to obtain high-resolution imagery, playing a crucial role in enhancing the precision of image analysis. However, existing methods suffer from an inherent trade-off between performance and efficiency, and their over-reliance on low-level features leads to insufficient fusion of multiscale information, low detail fidelity in reconstructed results, along with overfitting and artifacts. To address these issues, this article proposes a novel hierarchical frequency–spatial interaction network (HiFi-Net) for RSI SR. The proposed HiFi-Net incorporates a frequency–spatial attention module that leverages Fourier transformation to efficiently model global dependencies, thereby enhancing perceptual scope while reducing computational complexity. Furthermore, HiFi-Net employs a hierarchical interaction mechanism, comprised of a hierarchical spatial cross-attention (HSCA) module and a hierarchical channel cross-attention module, to facilitate cross-level feature modulation. This design effectively integrates low-level high-frequency details with high-level features, thereby adaptively aggregating critical information and resulting in superior SR performance. Extensive experiments on several public remote sensing datasets demonstrate that HiFi-Net consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both quantitative metrics and visual quality.
author: Yunong Chen, Zhenfeng Shao, Josue Antonio Nescolarde Selva, John van Genderen, Deren Li

title: MCAFNet: Multiscale Cross-Attention Fusion Network for HSI and LiDAR Data Joint Classification
content: Hyperspectral images (HSIs) and light detection and ranging (LiDAR) data provide complementary spatial–spectral and elevation information, respectively, which can significantly enhance land cover classification. However, existing methods are often limited by shallow cross-modal interactions and static architectures that struggle with multiscale complex scenes. This article proposes a multiscale cross-attention fusion network (MCAFNet) for joint classification of HSI and LiDAR data. To address the issues of insufficient feature interaction and weak scale adaptability in existing multimodal methods, MCAFNet adopts a three-branch architecture: a LiDAR branch for extracting spatial elevation features, an HSI branch for capturing global spectral features, and a multimodal branch that fuses local features through a multiscale feature enhancement (MSFE) module. The MSFE module extracts spatial–spectral features using depthwise separable and multiscale dilated convolutions, and enhances them via adaptive channel weighting, enabling dynamic multiscale feature perception. To achieve deep feature interaction, a multihead bidirectional cross-attention (MBCA) module is proposed, which realizes deep bidirectional interaction through feature reorganization and cross-branch attention, and introduces triple residual connections to preserve critical information. Extensive experiments conducted on multiple datasets demonstrate that MCAFNet outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods across all evaluation metrics. The source code will be available at https://github.com/2265526/MCAFNet
author: Junhui Cai, Xiaohui Huang, Xiaofei Yang, Jiangtao Peng, Yifang Ban, Yicong Zhou

title: Cross-Domain Self-Training Network With Prototype Rectification for Few-Shot Hyperspectral Image Classification
content: In recent years, the classification research of hyperspectral image (HSI) with scarce labeled samples has made significant progress, demonstrating strong performance when labeled samples are limited. However, current methods often overlook the importance of class prototypes. In addition, while conditional domain adversarial adaptation (CDAA) is employed to mitigate domain shift, it fails to account for the varying degrees of data distribution discrepancies across different classes, leading to suboptimal alignment. To address these issues, a cross-domain self-training network with prototype rectification (CSNPR) for few-shot HSI classification is proposed. First, a 3-D ghost attention network with Brownian distance covariance (TGAN-B) is introduced to improve the representation learning of HSI, generating more effective class prototypes. Next, an adaptive weighted CDAA (ACDAA) strategy is proposed, which assigns weights based on the domain discrepancy for better alignment. Finally, a pseudo label selection (PLS) module is developed to select the most confident pseudo-labeled samples from each class in the target domain (TD), thereby enriching the training set. The experimental findings derived from the analysis of three publicly available HSI datasets prove that the CSNPR method outperforms existing state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods, achieving superior and stable performance even with sparse labeled samples. Our source code is available at https://github.com/Yzh190-hue/CSNPR
author: Qingyan Wang, Peng Luo, Junping Zhang, Zhenhang Yao, Yujing Wang

title: Frequency-Domain Heterogeneous Rank-Entropy Bipolarization Network for Remote Sensing Change Detection
content: Recently, deep neural networks have been extensively investigated for remote sensing image change detection and have achieved remarkable performance improvements. However, most change detection methods do not effectively disentangle content and style features, making them vulnerable to domain shifts caused by illumination and seasonal variations, thereby producing pseudo-changes. Moreover, existing methods tend to overfit background cues in uncertain regions, causing the decision boundary to rely excessively on background textures and introducing background-induced spurious correlations. To address these issues, we propose a frequency-domain heterogeneous rank-entropy bipolarization network (FHRB-Net) for change detection. Specifically, we propose a frequency-domain heterogeneous-modulation fusion module (FHFM) that enhances change information and suppresses style interference through heterogeneous frequency-aware modulation, and employs a cosine-gated fusion mechanism to enhance bi-temporal feature consistency and alleviate domain shifts. Furthermore, a rank-entropy bipolarization loss (REB-Loss) is designed to quantify uncertainty from the model’s predicted probability map using a rank-based cumulative distribution function and Riemann-sum approximation and to reduce uncertainty by polarizing the probability distribution toward bipolar extremes to suppress background-induced spurious correlations. Experiments on several challenging remote sensing change detection (RSCD) datasets demonstrate that the proposed FHRB-Net yields the best overall performance. The code is publicly available at: https://github.com/lrz2025/FHRB-Net
author: Hang Sun, Runzhou Li, Chenyang Wang, Lefei Zhang, Dong Ren, Wenbin Wang

title: Spatial-Semantic Alignment and Change-Aware Network for Remote Sensing Image Change Captioning
content: Remote sensing image change captioning aims to generate natural language descriptions of dynamic changes between bitemporal images. This task faces challenges of aligning high-level semantics with sparse change regions and modeling long-range dependencies in diverse change patterns. To alleviate these limitations, we propose the spatial-semantic alignment and change-aware network, dubbed SACNet. It introduces a semantic-spatial collaborative encoding module synergizing a CNN-based spatial encoder with a CLIP-based semantic encoder, using precise spatial cues to refine global semantic features. A change-driven adaptive feature calibration module emphasizes discriminative change signals via intertemporal feature dissimilarity modeling, suppressing background noise and highlighting key changes. In addition, a multipath state space model (SSM)-guided spatial representation module is designed to capture diverse complex spatial change topologies. Extensive experiments on the LEVIR-CC and WHU-CDC datasets demonstrate that SACNet achieves state-of-the-art performance, generating both high-precision textual descriptions and approximate change masks. Code will be available at https://github.com/CVer-Yang/SACNet
author: Zhigang Yang, Huiguang Yao, Junzhen Wu, Linmao Tian, Weiping Ni, Qiang Li, Qi Wang

title: PI-SGF: Physics-Informed Swin-GeoFormer for Unsupervised Pore Aspect Ratio Prediction in Reservoir Characterization
content: Pore aspect ratio ( $\alpha $ ) is a key microstructural parameter that controls elastic wave propagation in reservoir rocks, yet it cannot be directly measured from well logs, and existing estimation methods rely on computationally expensive iterative inversion or pseudo-labels. This article presents physics-informed Swin-GeoFormer (PI-SGF), a physics-informed framework that couples log-sequence learning with the Berryman–Gassmann rock-physics model as a differentiable constraint, enabling fully unsupervised learning of  $\alpha $  alongside supervised estimation of porosity ( $\phi $ ), clay content ( $V_{\text {clay}}$ ), and water saturation ( $W_{S}$ ). The framework further incorporates geology-sensitive sequence modeling and progressive constraint strengthening to preserve formation-boundary information and stabilize the coupled supervised–unsupervised optimization. On strict cross-basin blind tests spanning three geologically distinct basins on two continents (Junggar, Sichuan, and North Sea), PI-SGF achieves  $R^{2} \geq 0.90$  for  $\alpha $  against independent inversion-derived references and velocity reconstruction  $R^{2} \gt 0.95$ . These results indicate that  $\alpha $  can be estimated from conventional logs in a physically consistent manner under the evaluated cross-basin setting.
author: Wenbiao Yang, Wenli Shang, Lianhai Wang, Zhiquan Liu

title: MSTFusion: Coarse-Resolution Change Guided Spatiotemporal Fusion for Fine-Resolution Land Surface Dynamics
content: Spatiotemporal fusion (STF) of multisource remote sensing imagery is widely used to generate observations with high spatial and temporal resolution for monitoring land surface dynamics. Although many existing STF methods perform well in reconstructing long-term phenological dynamics, their accuracy degrades markedly in the presence of abrupt land cover changes and spurious changes caused by clouds and cloud shadows. We propose multi-scale temporal fusion (MSTFusion), a change-guided STF method that integrates spatiotemporal change information from coarse-resolution time series with spatial details from high-resolution imagery. MSTFusion models the coarse-resolution time series in the graph Fourier domain, decomposing the observations into low-frequency trend-related components and high-frequency transient components, thereby helping distinguish persistent land cover changes from short-lived, spatially inconsistent perturbations induced by clouds and shadows. Based on this decomposition, MSTFusion derives a temporal change prior and learns a change map that selectively emphasizes genuine changes while suppressing spurious changes. It then uses this map to guide residual refinement with high-resolution spatial details. Experiments on three datasets across different temporal scales show that MSTFusion outperforms existing methods in regions with genuine land cover change, and in cloud-contaminated areas produces reconstructions that more faithfully represent underlying land surface dynamics while achieving quantitative performance comparable to state-of-the-art methods.
author: Sai Wang

title: A Novel Conditional Alignment and Pseudolabel Generation Method for Cross-Scene Hyperspectral Image Classification
content: A key bottleneck in cross-scene hyperspectral image (HSI) classification comes from domain shift, which poses a significant challenge for applying network models to some new, unlabeled scenes. Unsupervised domain adaptation, which aims to align the distributions of the source and target domains, has emerged as a powerful technique to address this challenge. In this article, we propose a novel method named conditional alignment and pseudolabel generation, which mainly consists of three contributions. First, to address the issue of misaligned conditional distributions between domains, a conditional maximum direction discrepancy (CMDD) strategy is proposed, which explicitly models the directional discrepancy of interdomain category features. By minimizing CMDD, the alignment of domain conditional distributions can be achieved. Second, spectral pseudolabel (SpePL) and spatial pseudolabel (SpaPL) are designed to fully learn the spectral and spatial information of the target domain. The SpePLs can also provide high-quality label guidance for CMDD in the early stages of training. Third, to address the alignment problem caused by spatial resolution mismatch across domains, a pixel patch unified network was constructed, which enables sufficient transfer of spatial information under varying resolutions and ensures the more efficient generation of the SpaPLs. A comparative evaluation of the proposed method with some state-of-the-art methods was conducted on three cross-scene HSI datasets. The experimental results show that our proposed method achieves excellent performance on all three datasets.
author: Cuiping Shi, Shihang Ding, Zhipeng Zhong, Yanmin Shuai, Keyan Wang

title: An Integration of Phenological Pattern and Satellite Image Time Series for Zanthoxylum bungeanum Maxim. Mapping Using Deep One-Class Classification
content: As a commercially and medicinally significant species extensively cultivated across Asia, Zanthoxylum bungeanum Maxim. (ZBM) demands precise spatial mapping to underpin regional economic development and ecological management. Although satellite image time series (SITS) coupled with machine learning have advanced crop-specific identification, accurately delineating ZBM in mountainous smallholder landscapes remains problematic. Similar agronomic practices and microclimatic variability intensify spectral confusion between ZBM and its cocultivated counterparts, while shifting agricultural policies and diverse crop rotations complicate the establishment of fixed classification schemes and render manual annotation prohibitively laborious. To address these challenges, this study proposes the phenology-stage aware temporo-spatial fusion transformer with deep one-class loss (PTSFOC), a novel framework for high-precision ZBM mapping using SITS data. PTSFOC addresses spectral ambiguity through the multi-phenology-stage feature extraction branches and cross-phenology-stage fusion module, which jointly model spatiotemporal signatures across ZBM’s key growth phases (e.g., fruit expansion and ripening) to enhance discriminative capacity. To alleviate labeling burdens, a deep one-class risk estimator trains the network using only positive and unlabeled samples, obviating the need for negative class definitions. Applied to Jiangjin District in Chongqing Municipality, a typical mountainous smallholder region, PTSFOC achieves an F1-score of 0.952, outperforming existing one-class classification methods. This work not only delivers a robust tool for mapping ZBM in complex agricultural landscapes but also offers a transferable strategy for other specialty crops plagued by spectral ambiguity, thereby informing targeted agricultural and ecological policy.
author: Yizhen Zheng, Wen Dong, Xin Zhang, Yihang Lu, Yanni Dong, Yu Chen, Tao Wu, Lin Li

title: A Bézier Curve-Based Progressive TIN Densification Filtering Algorithm for Airborne LiDAR Data in Complex Transmission Corridor Terrains
content: Accurate extraction of ground points from light detection and ranging (LiDAR) data is a fundamental step in raw data processing and a prerequisite for safety-critical transmission corridor inspections. However, transmission corridors often traverse rugged, vegetated, and fragmented terrain with irregular planimetric shapes, complicating ground-point filtering. To address these issues, this article proposes a Bézier curve-based progressive triangulated irregular network (TIN) densification algorithm (BPTD), which refines the conventional progressive TIN densification (PTD) framework in both TIN initialization and densification. Specifically, a high-quality initial TIN is constructed by flexibly inserting simulated ground points to better conform to complex corridor geometries. Building upon this foundation, the TIN is iteratively refined using a Bézier curve-guided dynamic angle threshold, which effectively mitigates potential misclassification issues associated with fixed thresholds under complex terrain conditions. Experiments conducted on transmission corridor and ISPRS benchmark datasets demonstrates that BPTD delivers consistently superior performance. In corridor scenarios, it achieves the lowest average total error (11.98%) with a Type Ⅰ error (9.75%), outperforming advanced PTD variants and the five widely used classical filtering algorithms. On ISPRS benchmark datasets, BPTD attains a lower average Type Ⅰ error of 2.67% and a total error of 4.46% which are lower than those obtained by the classical PTD algorithm. In addition, BPTD yields a higher kappa coefficient (84.50%), confirming its robustness. These results underscore BPTD's potential to enhance the reliability of LiDAR-based transmission corridor monitoring and complex-terrain mapping.
author: Dong Xing, Lin Du, Zhiyuan Wu, Jian Yang, Wei Gao

title: A Turbidity-Classified Bathymetric Mapping Framework Synergizing ICESat-2 and Sentinel-2 for Coastal Turbid Water
content: Accurate bathymetric mapping in turbid coastal waters remains a major challenge for optical remote sensing due to the strong attenuation of light by suspended particulate matter. A novel synergistic approach that integrates ICESat-2 photon-counting lidar data with Sentinel-2 multispectral imagery was introduced to enhance bathymetric retrieval in complex environments. The key innovation is the development of a turbidity-classified inversion framework, which dynamically adapts to spatial variations in water optical properties by utilizing red-edge reflectance as a proxy for turbidity. The proposed method first corrects ICESat-2 depth bias errors via a Random Forest-based compensation model, then applies stratified bathymetric models trained for low-, medium-, and high-turbidity regimes. Experimental results from Haizhou Bay and the Pearl River Estuary demonstrate significant accuracy improvements, with R$^{2}$ values exceeding 0.84 in low- and medium-turbidity zones. A critical turbidity threshold was identified at a red-edge reflectance of 0.127, beyond which model performance declines markedly. This study provides a robust, transferable strategy for high-resolution bathymetric mapping in turbid nearshore waters, enhancing the operational capability of satellite-derived bathymetry in globally relevant and optically complex coastal regions.
author: Shurong Guo, Chunpeng Chen, Hua Su, Wenting Wu

title: CLIP-Guided Dynamic Feature Fusion With Relation-Enhanced Attention for Remote Sensing Image Captioning
content: Remote sensing image captioning is a multimodal task that aims to automatically generate descriptions for remote sensing images. However, the inherent scale diversity and semantic complexity of RSI pose significant challenges in semantic alignment and explicit modeling of complex scene structures. To solve these problems, a new contrastive language–image pretraining (CLIP)-guided dynamic feature fusion and attention-based relation-enhanced model is proposed, referred to as CDRE. Specifically, we first fine-tune the encoder of CLIP on remote sensing image datasets, thereby significantly improving its capability of image–text semantic alignment in remote sensing scenes. Subsequently, we employ a feature extraction module to obtain a set of region features and the fine-tuned CLIP image embeddings. In addition, we design a dynamic semantic fusion module, which adaptively integrates the image embeddings and region features to generate hierarchically and semantically consistent image descriptions. Meanwhile, to enable explicit modeling of complex relationships in the scene, we introduce the relation-enhanced attention (REA) mechanism by combining 14 predefined spatial orientation relation terms, which effectively capture and represent fine-grained spatial and semantic dependencies among different objects in the image. Finally, a Transformer integrated with the REA mechanism and multihead cross-attention is employed to enable deep interaction between visual and textual information, ultimately generating the final descriptive text. Extensive experimental results on three public remote sensing image captioning datasets (RSICD, UCM-Captions, and Sydney-Captions) demonstrate the superior performance of CDRE. The code will be publicly available online.
author: Haifeng Sima, Wenqing Jiang, JianLong Wang, Changchun Li, Mingliang Xu

title: PhysFormer: A Multiscale Transformer With Semianalytical Spectral Constraints for Deep-Water Satellite Derived Bathymetry
content: Most existing satellite derived bathymetry (SDB) methods generally face the problem of reduced reliability caused by signal attenuation when the water depth exceeds 20 m. To address these challenges in SDB, we propose PhysFormer, a multiscale Transformer that incorporates semianalytical spectral constraints comprising a hierarchical self-attention backbone and two complementary modules: 1) a multiscale input (MSI) module to encode local seabed texture and broader environmental structure, and 2) a parallel spectral-reconstruction branch (SRB) that, using a semianalytical ocean-optics forward model, reconstructs subsurface reflectance from the predicted depth and wavelength-dependent parameters to strengthen physical consistency and thus improve robustness when bottom signals are weak. We trained and tested the model on the French Frigate Shoals dataset and applied the trained model (without fine-tuning) directly to Saipan and Zhongye Reef to assess cross-region generalization. Experiments show that PhysFormer attains an overall root-mean-squared error (RMSE) of 0.8328 m on the French Frigate Shoals test set, about 22% lower than the best baseline; in the more challenging 20–30 m interval it achieves RMSE = 0.9052 m, representing an improvement of approximately 24.9% relative to the best-performing baseline deep learning model in this depth interval. Without fine-tuning, PhysFormer yields RMSEs of 1.4071 m (Saipan) and 1.7332 m (Zhongye Reef). Ablation experiments suggest that SRB provides a larger contribution than MSI to the 20–30 m performance gain under our controlled setting, while MSI offers complementary benefits. In summary, the integration of physical consistency within the multiscale transformer architecture offers a significant advancement in SDB accuracy and generalization, providing improved performance in the optically challenging deep-water band (20–30 m).
author: Mou Luo, Li Ye, Yiqun Ma, Sensen Chu, Liang Cheng

title: SSFNet: Spatial–Spectral Frequency-Aware Network for Hyperspectral Image Classification
content: Spatial–spectral modeling methods have achieved remarkable progress in hyperspectral image (HSI) classification. However, most existing approaches primarily emphasize spatial–spectral feature fusion or multiscale representation learning, while the intrinsic frequency-discriminative characteristics of spatial and spectral signals remain underexplored for coordinated local–global modeling. To address these issues, we propose a novel spatial–spectral frequency-aware network (SSFNet) for HSI classification. SSFNet adopts a dual-branch architecture, consisting of a frequency-aware spatial modeling (FASM) branch and a frequency-based spectral modeling (FBSM) branch. The spatial branch (FASM) enhances boundary sensitivity and global contextual consistency through explicit high–low frequency modulation, enabling improved spatial representation learning. The FBSM branch performs spectral frequency decomposition, where the low-frequency component captures smooth, large-scale spectral trends, and the high-frequency component models fine-grained variations and discriminative fluctuations. A gated fusion mechanism is further designed to adaptively integrate complementary frequency features across different scales. Extensive experiments on four benchmark datasets demonstrate that SSFNet consistently outperforms eight state-of-the-art methods in terms of classification accuracy and generalization capability. These results verify the effectiveness of explicit frequency-aware spatial–spectral modeling for HSI classification.
author: Guanliang Wan, Danqing Liu, Yunxin Liu, Tengyue Yang, Yanhui Guo

title: Improving Satellite Imagery Segmentation Using Multiple Sentinel-2 Revisits
content: In recent years, analysis of remote sensing data has benefited immensely from borrowing techniques from the broader field of computer vision, including the use of shared models pretrained on large datasets. However, satellite imagery has unique features that are not accounted for in traditional computer vision, such as the existence of multiple revisits of the same location. Here, we explore the best way to use revisits in the framework of fine-tuning pretrained remote sensing models. As a test case, we focus on the applied research question of power substation segmentation, which is representative of applied uses of pretrained models more generally. Through extensive tests of different multitemporal input schemes across diverse model architectures, we find that fusing representations from multiple revisits in the model’s latent space is superior to other methods of using revisits, including as a form of data augmentation. We also find that a SWIN transformer-based architecture performs better than U-Nets and ViT-based models. We verify the generality of our results on a separate building density estimation task. In total, this work equips remote sensing scientists with a general method for utilizing the unique temporal properties of their images.
author: Kartik Jindgar, Grace W. Lindsay

title: A Progressive Remote Sensing Cloud Removal With Enhanced Global Context
content: Cloud removal in remote sensing imagery is crucial for applications in agriculture, environmental monitoring, and disaster assessment. However, existing methods often struggle to balance local details with global context, leading to significant information loss and degraded accuracy in downstream tasks. In this article, we propose a cascaded encoder–decoder network for progressive cloud removal that enhances global context understanding. Our architecture progressively refines feature representations to simultaneously capture global contextual information and preserve fine-grained local details. By integrating a triplet attention module and a fusion block, we strengthen hierarchical feature learning, enabling more accurate and visually consistent reconstructions of cloud-free images. Extensive experiments on the T-CLOUD, RICE1, and RICE2 datasets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves competitive and consistent performance in terms of PSNR, SSIM, and perceptual metrics. Furthermore, results from downstream scene classification tasks show that the restored images generated by our approach better retain discriminative semantic information compared to state-of-the-art methods.
author: Wenqian Zhang, Xiaochuan Wang, Yahui Gao, Lin Qiao, Ruijun Liu

title: PolAlpha-Net: A Lithology Segmentation Network Guided by Polarimetric Decomposition Knowledge for Dual-Polarization SAR Images in Vegetated Areas
content: The classification of lithology through remote sensing technology has become increasingly vital for geological surveys and resource exploration. However, accurately classifying lithology in areas covered by vegetation remains a significant challenge. In this article, we leverage the robust penetration and coherence capabilities of ALOS-1 dual-polarization synthetic aperture radar (SAR) data to propose a new deep learning network named polarimetric alpha-guided network (PolAlpha-Net), guided by polarization decomposition knowledge. This network utilizes the polarimetric decomposition parameter to learn stable features, enabling high-precision lithology segmentation in vegetated areas. Our study utilized images from June, September, and November, all of which were heavily vegetated, and performed segmentation tasks across four categories, including a background category, using existing geologic maps as ground truth. The results demonstrate that PolAlpha-Net obtains superior performance, achieving a mean accuracy of 86.79%. Specifically, the accuracy for the four categories—background, $Q_{4}$, Arjnd, and $\gamma _{5}^{2(1)}$—were 77.32%, 94.19%, 85.93%, and 89.71%, respectively. These outcomes confirm that PolAlpha-Net is adept at learning stable features even under dense vegetation cover and possesses excellent generalization capabilities across different conditions. Consequently, lithologic mapping with PolAlpha-Net yielded results with impressive spatial continuity. The outcomes of this research confirm that integrating PolAlpha-Net with ALOS-1 dual-polarization SAR data produces high-quality lithological segmentation in vegetated areas, underscoring its practical application value in remote sensing and geological analysis.
author: Liming He, Zenghui Qin, Yongliang Tang, Jiuyang Cai, Chen Xue, Yu Liu, Zhixiang Liu

title: Point-DCA-Enhanced Deep Learning Network for Photon Signal Classification in UAV-Based Photon-Counting LiDAR Bathymetry
content: A photon-counting LiDAR system deployed on uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAVs) enables highly mobile, high-efficiency, high-resolution shallow-water bathymetry. Photon-counting LiDAR technology can significantly enhance underwater weak signal detection capabilities but concomitantly introduces substantial noise sources, severely interfering with the extraction of target photon signals; this necessitates denoising while precisely discriminating between water surface and bottom photons for refraction correction. To address these challenges, this study proposes a dual cross-attention enhanced PointNet++ network (Point-DCA) for classifying noise, water column, bottom, and vegetation photons in bathymetric data. By capturing cross-channel and hierarchical spatial information through its DCA mechanism, Point-DCA overcomes the limited feature fusion capacity of PointNet++’s native skip connections, significantly improving classification performance. To validate the framework, a purpose-built photon-classification dataset was developed through raw photon data decoding, manual annotation, and computation of neighborhood point features to validate the framework. Experimental results showed that, without additional features, Point-DCA achieved a 4.4% higher overall accuracy (OA), 12.8% improvement in mean F1-score (mF1), and 17.3% increase in mean intersection over union (mIoU) compared to PointNet++. After incorporating neighborhood point features, Point-DCA improved the OA to 97.8%, mF1 to 95.7%, and mIoU to 91.9%. Tests across four water-depth scenarios validated the efficacy of Point-DCA in end-to-end photon point cloud denoising and classification.
author: Hongtao Li, Linbang He, Haisen Li, Zhengyang Zhang, Zeru Cui

title: Hyperspectral Image Denoising and Destriping With Adaptive Learnable Subspace Coefficient Tensor Low-Rank Prior
content: To obtain the clean hyperspectral image (HSI) from the noisy HSI with mixed noise and stripe noise, this article proposes a new and effective adaptive learnable subspace coefficient tensor low-rank (ALSCTLR) model for HSI denoising and destriping based on the unified tensor modeling and subspace representation, which novelly proposes an adaptive and learnable subspace representation coefficient tensor nuclear norm (SRCTNN) for HSI and a dual-mode tensor low-fibered-rank prior term for stripe noise. First, based on the inherently strong spectral low-rank property of HSI, we apply the spectral low-rank decomposition of HSI into the spectral subspace to obtain the subspace representation coefficient (SRC), which not only is actually an order-3 tensor, but also can be preferably seen as the generalized transformed HSI by revisiting the spectral subspace low-rank decomposition of HSI from a novel generalized transform with learnable row orthogonal spectral basis matrix (ROSBM). Thus, we further exploit the mode-3 tensor low-fibered-rank prior of SRC to model its global 3-D structural correlations, which is novelly proposed as the SRCTNN prior term. More specifically, by comparing with the famous TNN based on the Fourier transform matrix for HSI modeling, which is not data-adaptive, not learnable, and has high computational complexity, the proposed SRCTNN based on ROSBM has many preferable properties, such as, data-adaptive, learnable, and lower computational complexity. In addition, to simultaneously exploit the physically strong structural and directional property of stripe noise in a unified tensor modeling framework, we particularly exploit the mode-2 and mode-3 tensor low-fibered-rank prior of stripe noise, which is novelly proposed as the dual-mode tensor low-fibered-rank prior term. Then, the proposed ALSCTLR model is solved by using the alternating direction method of multipliers. Finally, we show extensively experimental results to demonstrate the superiority of proposed ALSCTLR approach for HSI denoising and destriping.
author: Pengfei Liu, Yihang Du, Liping Sun, Nan Huang, Zhizhong Zheng, Liang Xiao

title: Cross-Modal Hierarchical Fusion Framework for Classifying Heterogeneous Spatial Patterns in Local Climate Zone Mapping
content: Accurate delineation of local climate zones (LCZs) is essential for comprehending urban thermal environments and supporting sustainable urban planning. Traditional LCZ mapping approaches often rely on single-modality data or perform only shallow-level fusion, which limits their capacity to represent intricate urban semantics. To address this limitation, we introduce cross-modal hierarchical fusion (CMHFuse), a new cross-modal hierarchical fusion framework for LCZ classification utilizing multimodal remote sensing data. CMHFuse independently extracts hierarchical features from multiple modalities (e.g., Sentinel-2 imagery, land use/land cover, and building height) using modality-specific encoders, where a channel-spatial attention module is integrated to emphasize semantically important features. A cross-attention module enables the primary modality to selectively integrate complementary information from auxiliary modalities. It is embedded into a Transformer-based architecture to form a hierarchical cross-modal fusion pipeline. Furthermore, a specific-to-fused feature enhancement strategy adaptively combines enhanced and primary modality features at the feature level to improve classification robustness. Comprehensive evaluations on datasets collected from four cities confirm that CMHFuse outperforms current approaches. Additional ablation analysis highlights the individual importance of each modality and verifies the robustness of the proposed fusion strategy.
author: Chen Su, Qingyan Meng, Linlin Zhang, Fuqing Zhang, Hantian Wu, Shihong Du, Xiuyuan Zhang, Alimujiang Kasimu, Sorin Herban

title: Postfire Vegetation Recovery Assessment Through Time Series Reconstruction of Vegetation Index
content: Postfire vegetation recovery is a complex process involving spatiotemporally heterogeneous changes in vegetation structure and physiology. While remote sensing offers extensive monitoring capabilities, retrieving consistent recovery trajectories is often challenging due to phenological variability, atmospheric contamination, and cloud cover. This study proposes a robust framework for assessing heterogeneous recovery by reconstructing the normalized difference vegetation index (NDVI) time series using a double logistic function fitting combined with an iterative optimization algorithm. This approach mitigates challenges associated with atmospheric noise and irregular sampling while enabling the derivation of phenological parameters representing annual vegetation dynamics. Based on the reconstructed NDVI time series, we defined key phenological metrics, such as peak NDVI, integrated NDVI, and baseline NDVI, to quantify recovery characteristics. Applied to a Sentinel-2 multitemporal dataset spanning seven years regarding the 2019 Goseong-Sokcho wildfire on the Korean Peninsula, the approach achieved high fitting accuracy. Experimental results revealed that the proposed recovery indices, particularly the baseline NDVI, captured distinct structural recovery characteristics, whereas conventional peak summer NDVI showed rapid saturation driven by understory regrowth. Furthermore, the temporal patterns of recovery indices enabled the classification of post-fire vegetation management types with 86.5% overall accuracy, distinguishing between natural recovery and artificial restoration strategies. The proposed framework serves as a robust and practical tool for the continuous monitoring of complex postfire vegetation dynamics over extensive areas.
author: Garam Lee, Sang-Eun Park

title: Hybrid Multihop Differential Transformer and Pyramid Gate Mechanism for Mars Hyperspectral Image Classification
content: With advances in deep-space exploration, hyperspectral imaging of the Martian surface has become feasible, making surface material classification a key tool for Mars observation. However, unlike terrestrial scenes, Martian mineral classes exhibit subtle spectral differences and highly similar spatial structures, resulting in strong spectral–spatial similarity among different materials and significantly limiting the effectiveness of existing methods. To address these issues, we propose a hybrid multihop differential Transformer with a pyramid gating (HMDTPG) mechanism for Mars hyperspectral image classification. The key novelty lies in the multihop differential Transformer, which explicitly models spectral differences via a multihop differential attention mechanism, enhancing sensitivity to subtle mineral variations while preserving global spectral dependencies. In parallel, a spatial pyramid gated enhancement module is designed to capture discriminative features across multiple scales, suppress redundant information, and retain fine-grained discriminative spatial features through gating. Furthermore, a spectral–spatial adaptive fusion module is designed to enable deep interaction between spectral and spatial features and perform adaptive fusion. Experimental results show that hybridmultihop differential Transformer and pyramid gate mechanism outperforms other state-of-the-art methods on three public Mars hyperspectral imagery (MHSI) datasets, achieving overall accuracies of 98.32%, 97.34%, and 97.42% on Holden Crater, Nili Fossae, and Utopia Planitia datasets, respectively.
author: Haizhu Pan, Rui Liu, Haimiao Ge, Cuiping Shi, Ruixiang Zhao, Hui Yan, Moqi Liu

title: Deep Edge-Aware and Multiscale Fusion Network for Accurate Building Extraction in Remote Sensing Images
content: Theaccurate identification and extraction of buildings from high-resolution remote sensing images is crucial for urban planning, smart city development, land management, and GIS updating. However, building extraction remains challenging due to unclear boundaries, fragmented contours, and multiscale variability caused by occlusions, shadows, and diverse building shapes and sizes. This article proposes ME-UNet, an improved U-Net architecture designed to enhance edge representation, multiscale feature integration, and long-range spatial modeling. The architecture introduces three key modules: the multiscale feature fusion (MSFF) module, the edge-enhanced attention module (EEAM), and the lightweight spatial dependence module (LSDM). MSFF integrates multilevel features using multiscale convolutions and pooling operations, EEAM strengthens the focus on edge regions through reverse attention and edge guidance, and LSDM efficiently models long-range contextual interactions and improves structural consistency in building extraction. The module performs efficient directional context aggregation along the horizontal and vertical axes, thereby enhancing spatial dependence modeling without introducing excessive computational complexity. Extensive experiments on three public building segmentation datasets (WHU, Massachusetts, and Inria) demonstrate that ME-UNet consistently outperforms traditional U-Net models, achieving higher IoU and F1 scores, while maintaining a relatively lightweight design and improved robustness under high-noise conditions.
author: Long Li, Yang Cao, Hongfei Yu

title: SwinMSU: Swin Transformer V2-Based Multiscale Stepwise Up-sampling Feature Injection Network for Remote Sensing Image Fusion
content: Pan-sharpening aims to generate high-quality imagery by fusing the spatial details of panchromatic images with the spectral information of multispectral images. However, existing hybrid networks combining the convolutional neural network (CNN) and transformers often struggle to balance local feature extraction with global context decoding. To address this, we propose a Swin transformer V2-based multiscale stepwise up-sampling network (SwinMSU). SwinMSU employs a dual-branch architecture: a Swin-based branch captures global contextual representations, while a CNN branch focuses on local spatial features, achieving complementarity via multilevel injection. We introduce the group convolution weight smooth adjustment module, which applies adaptive smoothing regularization to enhance attention on key objects. In addition, double/triple hierarchical up-sampling modules are designed to progressively reconstruct high-resolution features while preserving high-frequency details. Experiments on WorldView-3, GaoFen-2, and QuickBird datasets demonstrate that SwinMSU achieves leading performance (QNR up to 0.9264, 0.9023, and 0.9050), outperforming state-of-the-art methods in both spatial fidelity and spectral consistency. The code can be available at RSIDEA-ECUT/SwinMSU.
author: Xinxiong Shi, Xunqiang Gong, Zhenyang Hui, Zechun Li, Yuanxin Ye, Yuting Wan, Ailong Ma, Yanfei Zhong

title: Global Ocean Significant Wave Height Retrieval From Spaceborne GNSS-R Data Using a CNN-Transformer Network
content: This study uses a deep learning method to retrieve significant wave height (SWH) from global navigation satellite system reflectometry data. To combine the advantages of convolutional neural networks and transformers, a hybrid network called CTWvHtNet is proposed. The model takes bistatic radar cross section and power analog parameters as key inputs and utilizes other auxiliary parameters to enhance SWH retrieval performance. In addition, a coordinate module is introduced to add positional encoding to the delay Doppler map, enabling the model to perceive the positional information of pixels in the feature map, and a self-attention assisted parameter feature extraction branch is devised to extract discriminative features from the auxiliary parameters. Evaluation results on the CYGNSS L1 V3.2 dataset demonstrate the model’s performance. Compared with the ERA5 data from the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts and buoy data from the National Data Buoy Center, the model achieves root mean square errors of 0.503 and 0.448 m, respectively, within the 0–8 m SWH range, with an inference speed of 0.0068 s. Besides, the retrieval performance in subdivided SWH intervals is analyzed, and the impact of different input parameters is discussed.
author: Jiacheng Zheng, Zili Li

title: Introducing Graph Attention Network to Improve Wang Zhengfei Semi-Empirical Model for Accurately Simulating Forest Fire Spread in Western Guizhou Province
content: Accurate simulation of forest fire spread is crucial for enhancing emergency response. Currently coupling the Wang Zhengfei model with cellular automata (CA) is regarded as an effective method for the simulation, but the rigid neighborhood rules inherent to CA often limit the ability to flexibly adapt to nonlinear fire propagation. To overcome this defect, this article utilized six major forest fires in western Guizhou province taking place on 20 February 2024, as a case study. We developed an improved model by training a graph attention network (GAT) on historical Guizhou fire incidents to adaptively adjust the CA’s neighborhood rules, utilizing the Wang Zhengfei spread rate as a key input feature. The fires were simulated and validated against the Sentinel-2 derived 20 m resolution burned patches via calculating a spatial agreement (SA) measure. In addition, the largest fire underwent dynamic assessment using the vegetation damage patterns (dNDVI) derived from high-frequency Gaofen-4 (GF-4) satellite observations. Results showed that SA measures from the GAT-improved model ranged from 0.713 to 0.990, much higher than those from the original Wang Zhengfei-CA model ranging from 0.095 to 0.773. And the dynamic simulation of the largest fire among the six was relatively consistent with the evolutionary patterns of vegetation damage characterized by GF-4 satellite observations derived normalized difference vegetation index (NDVI) changes. Overall, the GAT-improved model is a potentially effective framework for depicting the spatio-temporal evolution of forest fires in Guizhou province, providing a reliable tool for targeted forest fire prevention and suppression.
author: Yuying Gong, Jingyi Wang, Mingshi Li

title: PACA: 3-D Physical Adversarial Camouflage for Aircraft in Remote Sensing Under Multiview and Varying Weather Conditions
content: Most existing physical adversarial attack methods for remote sensing object detectors are optimized from a single nadir viewpoint, leading to significant performance degradation under multiview observations. Moreover, most methods directly overlay adversarial textures onto images during digital training without adequately modeling the color distribution of complex environmental backgrounds, causing severe color inconsistency between adversarial textures and their surroundings. This reduces visual realism, training stability, and environmental adaptability. To address these limitations, this article proposes physical full-coverage adversarial camouflage for aircraft (PACA), a framework designed to achieve robust deception across multiple viewpoints and complex environmental conditions. PACA integrates differentiable neural rendering with an environment-aware texture optimization mechanism: a neural rendering module (NeuroCam) enables precise texture mapping and multiview projection on 3-D aircraft surfaces, while an environment-aware module (feature integration and retrieval network) extracts and fuses environmental color features to generate visually consistent and physically realizable full-coverage adversarial textures. Extensive experiments on the high-fidelity simulated remote sensing dataset (RS-AirSim) and real-world physical prototype tests demonstrate that the proposed method effectively degrades detection performance under multiview observations. Compared with traditional remote sensing camouflage strategies, PACA reduces the detection AP from 0.30–0.60 to below 0.02 in most full-access settings, and maintains strong attack effectiveness in real-world physical deployments, while remaining robust to viewpoint variations and complex weather conditions. Source code, dataset, and 3-D simulation environment will be publicly available at our GitHub repository.
author: Jinpeng Luo, Gang Liu, Min Dang, Zhaolu Zheng, Xulong Zhao, Nan Luo

title: Phenological Stage-Specific Dynamic Estimation of Rice Aboveground Biomass With Remote Sensing and Flux Data
content: Aboveground biomass (AGB) serves as a crucial indicator for evaluating physiological life activities and monitoring growth status. However, the scarce measured data and the limited temporal sampling constrain the accurate estimation of AGB dynamics by existing models. These limitations also restrict the detailed characterization of rice growth processes and hinder systematic comparison between estimation accuracy across different phenological stage division schemes. Based on this, this study proposes a stage-partitioned, time-series estimation model for rice AGB based on remote sensing and flux data. By focusing on rice growth characteristics, the model constructs a dynamic monitoring framework for AGB across phenological stages by dynamically optimizing phenology-sensitive feature parameters. First, leveraging time-series data from flux monitoring and taking into account the distinct growth characteristics exhibited during different phenological stages, key vegetation indices and environmental parameters are screened to characterize the rice growth process. Subsequently, the estimation accuracy achieved by multiple machine learning algorithms under two phenological stage division strategies is systematically compared to select the optimal estimation model for each phenological stage partitioning model. Finally, the improvement effect of the stage-partitioned modeling approach on AGB estimation accuracy is quantitatively evaluated. Quantitative evaluation demonstrates that, compared with the long short-term memory model applied to the entire growth cycle (R2 = 0.827, RMSE = 4.862 g/m2), both stage-partitioned approaches improved the estimation of rice AGB. Among them, the convolutional neural networks model using physiological function to partition growth stages showed the most significant improvement (R2 = 0.910, RMSE = 3.522 g/m2), and its performance was markedly superior to the model partitioned according to agricultural management practices (R2 = 0.851, RMSE = 4.435 g/m2). Validation with field-measured data from Changshu City further corroborated that the modeling approach partitioning phenological stages based on physiological function significantly reduced AGB estimation errors and exhibited superior stability. This approach provides an effective method for dynamic rice AGB monitoring and supports the development of precision management strategies in rice cultivation.
author: Yu Liu, Zuobin Ou, Xiao Ling, Shuwei Wang

title: Self-orthorectification of satellite images using multi-view geometric constraints without external geospatial data

title: Research Bits: May 19
content:
Programmable PIC

Researchers from the University of Washington designed a low-power programmable photonic integrated circuit that is electrically reconfigurable and can be mass-produced.

“This optical chip could help to accelerate the prototyping cycle while reducing power consumption for applications like AI computing. Our study is also the first time someone has shown that these kinds of optical circuits can be controlled with electrical signals, reliably and very accurately,” said Rui Chen, postdoctoral research associate at MIT and former doctoral student at UW, in a press release. “We built our circuit using common foundry processes, which demonstrates the scalability of the system.”

To reduce power consumption, the device uses phase-change materials that enable it to maintain its programmed or reconfigured state without a power supply.

Next, the team plans to build a larger-scale optoelectronic system containing the optical chip. “An important next step is to test this optical chip in some real applications,” Chen added. “We’d like to put this circuit in application scenarios, such as AI computing, optical switches in data center infrastructure, and optical sensing.” [1]

Small optical features in hydrogels

Researchers from Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) used a fabrication technique called implosion carving to create optical computing devices. The technique imprints 3D features throughout a hydrogel. The hydrogel is immersed in a photosensitizing dye, and a laser is used to create vacancies in the desired pattern. The hydrogel is then shrunk in a two-step process, reducing 800nm feature sizes to 100nm.

“In order to enable nanophotonic applications in visible light, we need to make nanostructures with feature sizes with a resolution less than 100 nanometers. Only in that way can we precisely create the structure that can manipulate visible light,” said Quansan Yang, assistant professor at the University of Washington and former MIT postdoc, in a press release.

The demonstration device was capable of performing a simple digit-classification task using a pattern of vacancies that diffracts input light as it passes through layers of patterned hydrogel, so that the output light was determined by the shape of the digit that was entered into the system. While the demonstration showed a simple neural network task, the team plans to use the same principles for high-throughput imaging techniques, such as optical devices that classify cells based on their state as they flow through a microfluidic device.

“One of the very attractive features of this technology is that you can manipulate the property of the material at every tiny location,” said Dushan Wadduwage, an assistant professor at Old Dominion University and former MIT postdoc, in a press release. “You have millions of different locations that you need to decide the property of, and that turns into a really interesting design problem where we can use deep-learning algorithms to find designs over these millions of parameters and come up with parts that go into optical systems in new ways.” [2]

On-chip UV generation

Researchers from Harvard University and the University of Twente were able to generate milliwatt-level UV light on a thin-film lithium niobate (TFLN) photonic chip. To produce the UV light, the team generated two red light photons before converting them to a single UV photon.

The team built a waveguide on the TFLN platform with electrodes brought up against the sidewalls to reverse the orientation of the material’s crystal structure periodically, up to a thousand times per millimeter. The alternating voltage on and off along the waveguide created the pattern that enables conversion.

In previous versions, the electrodes were placed further from the waveguide. “In our design, they sit right on it,” said Kees Franken, postdoc at UT and CEO of spin-off Sabratha, which is commercializing TFLN technology, in a statement. “That required a fabrication process accurate to fifty nanometers across a chip several centimeters long. But it gives us far more control, and the conversion from red to UV works much more efficiently.”

Potential applications include quantum technology, optical atomic clocks, and measurement equipment. [3]

References

[1] R. Chen, A. Tang, J. Dutta, et al. NEO-PGA: Nonvolatile electro-optically programmable gate array. Sci. Adv. 12, eaea9383 (2026). https://doi.org/10.1126/sciadv.aea9383

[2] Q. Yang, G. Yang, T. Nambara, et al. Isotropic shrinkage of patterned vacancies enables three-dimensional nanoprecise metastructures for visible light applications. Nat. Photon. (2026). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41566-026-01896-1

[3] C.A.A. Franken, S.S. Ghosh, C.C. Rodrigues, et al. Milliwatt-level UV generation using sidewall poled lithium niobate. Nat Commun 17, 3651 (2026). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-026-68524-y

The post Research Bits: May 19 appeared first on Semiconductor Engineering.

author: Linda Christensen

title: A Deionized Water-Based Large-Scale Transfer Process For 2D Materials Grown on Sapphire (AMO, RWTH, Aixtron)
content:
A new technical paper, “Water-based, large-scale transfer of 2D materials grown on sapphire substrates,” was published by researchers at AMO GmbH, RWTH Aachen University, and AIXTRON SE.

Abstract

“Two-dimensional materials (2DMs) hold significant potential for future electronics, as demonstrated by high-performing devices for sensing, optics, and electronics. However, scalable growth techniques such as metal-organic chemical vapor deposition (MOCVD) typically require high temperatures, which limit their integration with conventional semiconductor processes. Therefore, reliable transfer processes from crystalline, low-contamination growth substrates such as sapphire are essential. Existing methods using chemicals such as potassium hydroxide (KOH) have potential quality issues and require manual handling and enhanced safety precautions. Here, we introduce a deionized water-based, frame-assisted, large-scale transfer method for two widely used two-dimensional materials, molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) and hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN). We demonstrate the scalability of our process with the transfer of MoS2 from a 100 mm diameter sapphire wafer. The material quality was verified as-grown and after transfer via atomic force microscopy, scanning electron microscopy, and Raman spectroscopy. The data show less compressive strain levels and insignificant changes in the doping or contamination of 2DMs after transfer.”

Find the technical paper here. April 2026.

Rademacher, N., Völkel, L., Reato, E. et al. Water-based, large-scale transfer of 2D materials grown on sapphire substrates. npj 2D Mater Appl 10, 48 (2026). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41699-026-00696-z

The post A Deionized Water-Based Large-Scale Transfer Process For 2D Materials Grown on Sapphire (AMO, RWTH, Aixtron) appeared first on Semiconductor Engineering.
author: Technical Paper Link

title: Evaluating and Calibrating Performance On RISC-V Vector Processors (KTH, LLNL, BSC)
content:
A new technical paper, “Closer in the Gap: Towards Portable Performance on RISC-V Vector Processors,” was published by researchers at KTH Royal Institute of Technology, Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, and Barcelona Supercomputing Center.

Abstract

“The RISC-V Vector Extension~(RVV) is a cornerstone for supporting compute throughout in scientific and machine learning workloads. Yet compiler support and performance monitoring on real RVV~1.0 hardware are still evolving. In this work, we design a suite of assembly microbenchmarks to establish performance ceilings and calibrate performance counters on RVV hardware. Leveraging the assembly benchmarks, we find that predication overhead and stride load pose performance challenges that current compiler cost models do not yet fully address. Moreover, we present the first evaluation of GCC 15 and LLVM 21 autovectorization in HPC and ML proxy applications. GCC 15 outperforms LLVM 21 in four out of six applications. LLVM 21 only outperforms GCC 15 in SGEMM and DGEMM, driven by more aggressive instruction reduction confirmed through validated perf counters on the RVV hardware. We further show that the default LMUL selection in compilers performs close to the optimal. To study the RVV support for product-level application, we also evaluate the state-vector quantum simulator, Google’s Qsim, with both manual RVV intrinsics and compiler auto-vectorization, revealing immaturity in current RVV compiler for complicated memory access pattern.”

Find the technical paper here. May 2026.

Shi, Ruimin, Maya Gokhale, Pei-Hung Lin, Xavier Teruel, and Ivy Peng. “Closer in the Gap: Towards Portable Performance on RISC-V Vector Processors.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2605.10860 (2026).

The post Evaluating and Calibrating Performance On RISC-V Vector Processors (KTH, LLNL, BSC) appeared first on Semiconductor Engineering.

title: Scalable Photomask Optimization With Morphological Learning  (SUNY Buffalo, VU, IBM)
content:
A new technical paper, “MorphOPC: Advancing Mask Optimization with Multi-scale Hierarchical Morphological Learning,” was published by researchers at University at Buffalo, Villanova University, and IBM T. J. Watson Research Center.

Abstract

“As feature sizes shrink to the nanometer scale, accurately transferring circuit patterns from photomasks to silicon wafers becomes increasingly challenging. Optical proximity correction (OPC) is widely used to ensure pattern fidelity and manufacturability. Recent generative mask optimization models based on encoder-decoder architecture can synthesize near-optimal masks, serving as fast machine learning (ML) surrogates for traditional OPC. However, these models often fail to capture the geometric transformations from target layouts to mask patterns, leading to suboptimal quality. In this work, we formulate mask generation as a sequence of morphological operations on local layout features and propose MorphOPC, a multi-scale hierarchical model with neural morphological modules to learn these transformations. Experiments on edge-based OPC and ILT benchmarks across metal and via layers show that MorphOPC consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving higher printing fidelity and lower manufacturing cost, demonstrating strong potential for scalable mask optimization.”

Find the technical paper here. April 2026.

Hu, Yuting, Lei Zhuang, Chen Wang, Ruiyang Qin, Hua Xiang, Gi-joon Nam, and Jinjun Xiong. “MorphOPC: Advancing Mask Optimization with Multi-scale Hierarchical Morphological Learning.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2605.12528 (2026).

The post Scalable Photomask Optimization With Morphological Learning (SUNY Buffalo, VU, IBM) appeared first on Semiconductor Engineering.

title: Workflow-Level Design For Trustworthy GenAI Integration in Vehicles (UOL, Denso)
content:
A new technical paper, “Workflow-Level Design Principles for Trustworthy GenAI in Automotive System Engineering,” was published by researchers at University of Oldenburg and Denso Automotive.

Abstract

“The adoption of large language models in safety-critical system engineering is constrained by trustworthiness, traceability, and alignment with established verification practices. We propose workflow-level design principles for trustworthy GenAI integration and demonstrate them in an end-to-end automotive pipeline, from requirement delta identification to SysML v2 architecture update and re-testing. First, we show that monolithic (“big-bang”) prompting misses critical changes in large specifications, while section-wise decomposition with diversity sampling and lightweight NLP sanity checks improves completeness and correctness. Then, we propagate requirement deltas into SysML v2 models and validate updates via compilation and static analysis. Additionally, we ensure traceable regression testing by generating test cases through explicit mappings from specification variables to architectural ports and states, providing practical safeguards for GenAI used in safety-critical automotive engineering.”

Find the technical paper here. February 2026.

Cheng, Chih-Hong, Brian Hsuan-Cheng Liao, Adam Molin, and Hasan Esen. “Workflow-Level Design Principles for Trustworthy GenAI in Automotive System Engineering.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2602.19614 (2026).

The post Workflow-Level Design For Trustworthy GenAI Integration in Vehicles (UOL, Denso) appeared first on Semiconductor Engineering.

title: HW-Native, GPU Compiler for Large-scale ML Production Systems (UC San Diego, Meta)
content:
A new technical paper, “TLX: Hardware-Native, Evolvable MIMW GPU Compiler for Large-scale Production Environments,” was published by researchers at UC San Diego and Meta.

Abstract

“Modern GPUs increasingly rely on specialized hardware units and asynchronous coordination mechanisms, so performance depends on orchestrating data movement, tensor-core computation, and synchronization rather than exposing more thread-level parallelism. This creates a programming-model tension: if too much execution structure is hidden, the compiler must catch up to new hardware mechanisms; if too much is exposed, the burden of orchestration falls back onto the programmer. We present TLX (Triton Low-level Language Extensions), built around MIMW (Multi-Instruction, Multi-Warp), which expresses orchestration at warp-group granularity while preserving Triton’s productive blocked programming model for regular computation. TLX realizes this idea as an embedded extension to Triton, exposing explicit interfaces for multi-warp execution, local-memory orchestration, asynchronous operations, and cluster-aware control. Our evaluation shows that TLX supports substantial customization with limited development effort while remaining competitive with state-of-the-art implementations. TLX-authored kernels have been deployed in large-scale training and inference production systems.  Our code is open sourced at this https URL.”

Find the technical paper here. May 2026.

Guan, Yue, Hongtao Yu, Peng Chen, Daohang Shi, Karthik Manivannan, Nicholas J. Riasanovsky, Manman Ren et al. “TLX: Hardware-Native, Evolvable MIMW GPU Compiler for Large-scale Production Environments.” arXiv preprint arXiv:2605.10905 (2026).

The post HW-Native, GPU Compiler for Large-scale ML Production Systems (UC San Diego, Meta) appeared first on Semiconductor Engineering.
